title: In the shadow of minority rights: decolonising gender, liberalism and the politics of difference [Book Review]
author: Rodrigues

opening_move.description: The review opens by framing Dutta's book as a reframing of political agency in contemporary India: Muslim women navigate a landscape shaped by legal pluralism and communal politics through embodied ethical practice, not merely through liberal state claims.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The liberal state is not the locus of justice for Muslim women in India; their political agency is enacted through everyday ethical practices within lived Islamic and constitutional traditions

theoretical_positioning.description: The review presents Dutta's postcolonial feminist argument appreciatively, foregrounding the book's empirical grounding in fieldwork with Muslim women activists.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: fieldwork with Muslim women activists (BMMA, MCMT) in Mumbai
data_sources: fieldwork with Bharatiya Muslim Mahila Andolan (BMMA)
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review of an empirically-grounded work. The reviewed book's fieldwork is constitutive of its argument; the review presents this without independent empirical contribution.

case_type.case_name: Muslim women activists, BMMA and MCMT, Mumbai, India

confidence_notes: Book review genre. This is the only review in the corpus that presents a book with constitutive ethnographic evidence — the fieldwork drives the argument, not illustrates it.
